questions.question: What is the time complexity of Bubble Sort algorithm?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
questions.explanation: Bubble sort is a simple sorting algorithm that works by repeatedly swapping the adjacent elements if they are in the wrong order. The time complexity of bubble sort is O(n) in the best case scenario, O(n^2) in the average case scenario, and O(n^2) in the worst case scenario.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Bubble Sort algorithm is true?
options: Bubble Sort is a comparison-based sorting algorithm.
Bubble Sort is an in-place sorting algorithm.
Bubble Sort is a stable sorting algorithm.
Bubble Sort is not a sorting algorithm.
questions.explanation: Bubble sort is indeed an in-place sorting algorithm, meaning it can be implemented using a minimum amount of extra memory.

questions.question: What is the advantage of using Bubble Sort algorithm over Quick Sort?
options: Bubble Sort is faster for small datasets.
Bubble Sort is more stable than Quick Sort.
Bubble Sort is suitable for nearly sorted data.
Bubble Sort is not suitable for large datasets.
questions.explanation: One advantage of bubble sort over quicksort is that it is more suitable for nearly sorted data, as it has low overhead and can take advantage of existing order.

questions.question: Why is Bubble Sort algorithm not suitable for large datasets?
options: Bubble Sort algorithm has high overhead.
Bubble Sort algorithm has low overhead.
Bubble Sort algorithm is not efficient for large datasets.
Bubble Sort algorithm is stable for large datasets.
questions.explanation: One reason bubble sort algorithm is not suitable for large datasets is because of its O(n^2) time complexity, which can lead to inefficient performance for large inputs.

questions.question: What is the worst-case scenario of Bubble Sort algorithm?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
questions.explanation: The worst-case scenario of bubble sort algorithm is O(n^2), which occurs when the input array is sorted in reverse order.
